children.name: Rhipidura
children.description: The fantails — a large genus of small, active insectivores named for their long, fanned tails that are constantly in motion, flicked, cocked, and spread in a characteristic display. They are found from South Asia through Australasia to the Pacific islands. Their restless foraging behaviour involves continuous movement through the understorey, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Rhipidura leucophrys
children.description: Australia's most familiar fantail, a boldly patterned black-and-white bird with a long, constantly wagging tail, found across Australia, New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands. It is bold and confiding, often approaching humans closely in gardens and parks. Its aggressive, chattering calls are directed at intruders far larger than itself, and it is one of the few small birds that will actively mob raptors.

children.name: Rhipidura albiscapa
children.description: A small, active grey fantail with a prominent white eyebrow and a long, fanned tail that it constantly spreads and flicks while foraging, found across Australia, New Caledonia, and the Solomon Islands. It is a hyperactive insectivore that twists and turns through the air in pursuit of prey, fanning its tail at every change of direction. Its thin, piping song is a constant background sound in Australian forests.

children.name: Rhipidura fuliginosa
children.description: One of New Zealand's most beloved birds, a small fantail with a broadly fanned tail that is dark above and white-tipped, found in both the North and South Islands. It is famous for its charming, approachable nature, often fluttering close to humans in forests and gardens. Its constant, acrobatic foraging and cheerful 'cheep-cheep' call make it a favourite among New Zealand birdwatchers.

children.name: Rhipidura rufifrons
children.description: A strikingly coloured fantail with a rufous rump, tail base, and forehead, a white throat, and a black-and-white breast band, found in forests of eastern Australia and Pacific islands. It is a restless, lissom forager that fans its tail almost constantly while flitting through the understorey. It is a long-distance migrant within Australia, moving northward in winter.

children.name: Rhipidura rufiventris
children.description: A warm grey-brown fantail with a rufous belly and a pale throat, found in northern Australia, New Guinea, and the Lesser Sundas. It inhabits mangroves, monsoon forests, and woodlands, foraging actively in the lower and mid-storey. Its song is a series of simple, thin, descending whistles.
